author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: oh some info on why the taulman is so awesome? its a mixture of nylon and other materials with the 915 being the stiffest and hardest to deform and snap, being Polycarbonate and nylon as part of the mix :)

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: really wish you could get a partnership with Taulman and then print some of these "production testing" level products using their 915 or at least the 230 nylon filaments (the 230 can be printed with any printer that can print ABS and PLA, and is in fact easier than even PLA to print, honestly just have to keep the filament dry as it absorbs moisture from the air then gets really difficult to print and stinks a bit, but a box with a small hole on each end and an electric heater blowing into it for about 4 hours will take care of even the worst issues of moisture in the filament :) just blow hot air in and it will dry it right  up. I ran mine at the medium setting during the summer and low heat during the winter and it dried my filament out right quick then I was able to use the filament with it out in the air for like 3 days before having to dry it out again :)

author: @stewartpalmer2456
text: This may have been suggested already. If you leave the pin plastic, but with a shaft hole, you can simply slide a pin in it and super glue it in place. Metal pins surrounded by plastic sleeve glued for strength. I too have had this stuff shear off unexpectedly. You might try to merge the layers with a melting dip. ABS requires Acetone and PLT requires alcohol. But I love what you are doing here.

author: @Staedford
text: Could you have the blades folding forward (if they really must be designed to fold). I understand that folding back is neater for stowage. Another option could be to have the blades detachable, so for stowage the blades sit as they presently sit when folded back, but for generating, you detach from the hub & turn 180deg and clip back on to the hub but now they cant fold back due to the bracket design.

I would question the longevity of that gearbox (especially the faster end) given that the gears don't have bearings.  Adding them will make it quieter and prevent the pins from wearing out.

author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
text:
Simplest way to get rid of a vane is to put the turbine on the REAR of the generator.

The folding turbine MAY actually INCREASE the efficiency.

If you think about the fact that the blades weigh something, IF they are actually NOT popping up along the TOP side [PARTICULARLY with the blades in "DRAG" mode (as opposed to "PROW" mode...)] this CAN potentially ADD a tiny touch of improved performance!

In other words, by NOT needing to LIFT the blade assembly, you MIGHT be able to recover about 20% more power.

Seeing as gravity, air drag, pressure AND centrifugal forces SHOULD cause the blades to hurry up and rush out into peak torque, once they get past a certain point.

You may want to investigate this a little bit.

Looking at some of your other works, this SHOULD become easily evident to you AND be something you find rather amusing, if not quite compelling.

Again, should this have been forgotten since the start of the post, this REALLY, and ONLY works most effectively, if the blades are being used TRAILING the main housing and pivot point of the system.

The reason for blades to the rear is 1, the potential power gains from a collapsing blade function, but 2, the fact that is dispenses with the need for a vane to the back of the unit, therefore simplifying the overall structure and making it ever so slightly compact. 

[again, addressing many of the details with pusher prop airplanes, where aerodynamics favor increased efficiency and steering acuity, where the power is rear of C.O.G.]

Just a little thought to put out there for ya.

author: @russellzauner
text:
I am not an affiliate but I've used some interesting materials (available as 3DP filament, some of them) from a company called Igus.  They specialize in something called "tribological" family of engineered materials as well as extremely long wear, self lubricating, and high strength versions for replacing steel in industrial bearing applications.
They also offer bar and powder stock as well as sheet/tapes in some.  They are usually excited to work on interesting projects and your time difference should be better than mine in the US as they're based in Germany.  They have US sales reps but to talk to the home office is always a business day off, and I respect that - if your project can't wait for a day it's probably going to fail (has been my hard earned experience).  Much better to light up your sales reps phones when you have *that* situation and *never* otherwise (also hard earned experience).

author: @DFPercush
text: At last, the field test... awesome results!  Have you considered doing lost PLA casting with aluminum? There are a few different techniques. One of them is to make the first negative mold from silicone, use that to make a wax positive, then coat that with plaster, sand, etc. Then you can melt the wax and recover it, and do the next one a lot quicker than printing another one. Less carbon residue inside the mold that way too.

replies.author: @jasonburguess
replies.text: @RobertMurraySmith   I would advise one that is Not hydrostatic,  the mechanical ones should work better for this application,  also the differential should be allowed to stay a differential,  so the turbines can spin independently and at different speeds while still providing input rotation.  Another idea to try would be to use an old generator head off an old broken gasoline generator,  say 5-7kw and just use that as the power generation system,  it will turn slower than the rated power and output less, but should be more efficient than a diy solution.  If it'll make 5kw at 3500 rpms, it should at least make 500watts at say 200 rpms. Worth a try and a trip to the junkyard for sure. Love the videos and keep up the good work!

author: @jamest.5001
text: Multiple planets in a gear solar system is not unheard of. 3,4,5,6,8,9,12 would be acceptable,  using beatings would acccelerate the efficiency, and co$t. However pins and bushings, brass isn't cheap,  maybe stainless pins , polished to a mirror shine. Then use a steel bushing, ceramic coated  with a dry film lubricant.  Or stainless bushings with steel pins with a ceramic dry film lubricant, even  use of graphite,  on the pins and bushings , even the gears, they will look dirty, yet spin freely gears sliding past one another with ease,  possibly a fine sand paper hone of the bush and pin, in the 1500-2000 grit or better,  then rub in graphite under pressure, possibly try using heat and pressure, or a really thin glue, wipe 99% off, then dip in graphite powder . Use extreme pressure to get graphite in the tiny scratches left by the 1500-3000 grit  use even pressure around the pin, apply around 2000 psi , and possibly try the same without glue,  and use heat, around 200-400 + amps at  0.5-1v, held in a steel ( ) and graphite  blocks with grooves to fit the pin with with a ultra fine graphite powder, aroundthe pin, clamps on 3-4 sides. Then pass the current, heating the pin hopefully to the point where 1of 2 things happen.  Either the expanded steel allows the fine scratches to clamp on the graphite powder,  or the heated steel reaches a temperature high enough, that the steel router most layer is heated and softened enough that the graphite powder is entered into the outer layer, like tiny diamonds in a ring, then use a lathe or drill and file, to be certain the pin is flat and round ,  it should at the least have graphite stuck to it decently, making a slippery surface for a bushing to ride, or you just made a tiny pile of garbage!😆 Experiment and find out!!✌️

author: @jamesdim
text: Concerning the shear of the pin, another solution would be to apply CA glue and let it seep in the joints and cover the pin. It's really surprising how much more strength this offers to 3d printed stuff, especially when they're not printed in the ideal direction. I also use baking soda with the glue to maximize the strength and fill in the gaps more appropriately. Could even mix in some graphene!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: good idea mate - cheers

author: @skampp
text: Most people think of printing something like this either horizontally or vertically (rotating the part on the bed).  That's the right thought, but printing a part such as this at between 35 and 55 degrees would help tremendously.  It changes the shear angle, and for most printers, would only require support on the bottom section.  Anything beyond 55 degrees though would require supports everywhere.  That being said, pins are a better choice, nice one sir!  Thank you for the great series.

author: @piotr86
text:
Do not replace these pins with metal ones. Increase their diameter and enlarge the holes in the planets.
Regardless, disassemble the motor that spins the platter in the microwave. You will see that the gears that spin fast are paper thin. The gears that transmit more torque are thick. So the planetary gears in your generator don't have to be the same thickness in every stage.

author: @bikerfirefarter7280
text:
Excellent. Nice to see something real bringing several concepts together.
Please excuse me. Just curious, not criticising. You appear to have the blades with the convex side facing the oncoming wind. Wouldn't they generate more lift/torque with the concave face 'forward'. Or am I missing something obvious?
Thanks.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: this kind of blade actually works both ways round - because of the scoop in it is can either act in a drag fashion or as an airfoil - so it is not strictly one or the other mate

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Hi Rob,
I get that. But asymmetric airfoils are more efficient in one orientation/angle-of-attack than the other, Bernoulli principle aside. And ignoring the effects of airflow velocity isn't doing sonic daft stuff, there must be a more efficient orientation of one over the other regarding 'lift'. I know airplanes can fly upside down, but unless they have symmetric airfoil sections one way up is more efficient than the other at extracting defleted-energy from the airflow.
Am I still missing something obvious.?
Please explain it as if you are talking to an idiot.  ;-)
Thanks very much.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @bikerfirefarter7280 you are absolutely right mate - but this is made from soil pipe and that means compromises - aerodynamically not the best way to go but for price - difficult to beat

replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering The ring layer (on which the sheared pins stood) is 2/3rds open space. Could you not add walls from the base of that up to the next layer (which is pinned only by those 3 pins currently) and grossly increase the strength and resilience of the whole thing without resorting to metal pins? You'd need to glue those walls to the next level however.

author: @regularfryt
text: When I'm printing gears I usually use PETG rather than PLA.  The layer lines aren't nearly as weak, and it's got a little bit more flex in it to absorb the odd dings it gets.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice advice - cheers mate

author: @AdrianCubas1980
text: Hi Rob, I don't think using so many gears is a viable solution. From the point of view of efficiency, it is a waste of energy and the cause of many mechanical failures in this type of 3D creations. Best would be to use a rewound bldc motor, with a KV (RPM/V) between 20-50. It is necessary to promote that the windings have the lowest possible resistance (ideally less than 1 ohm). If you use a three-phase rectifier with Schotkky diodes, much better.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I understand mate - thanks for the advice and cheers - i should add I am going to ignore it but not out of disrespect for what you say - i think you are spot on but i have other objectives beyond just making a wind turbine that i also include in the videos.

author: @baraBober

replies.author: @baraBober
replies.text: @johnschneider931 Nylon is self lubricating, and is used widely in the industry. Most of the gears in your printer are made out of nylon, AND they're lubricated with a special grease

author: @perpetual4958
text:
4.5 M/s is quite a lot. During the summer one needs to be right at the North Sea coast to get those speeds!

So what would it take to make this a Fan. Either more speed tollerant, or even higher torque fors pushing High Volume Low Speed. Direct drive moters have become the norm in HVLS.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: the Beaufort scale calls that a moderate breeze mate - it's not a lot at all - we get that and more most every evening where i live

replies.author: @perpetual4958
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Beaufort 2-3. Coastal region on otherside of the North Sea that is common windspeed, during the summer (this year is extra quiet, BTW), land inward is drops to half. Outside the summer, when one wants to remain inside, it doubles.

But at this cut-in speed it shows why smaller windturbines/mills do not make sense, unless they can be construed extremely cheap. And that is where you work.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @perpetual4958 cheers mate

@ThinkingandTinkering Always, Robert. Making things cheaply, cleverly, but tough is where it is at.

BTW, still looking for Builders Board, no-one  seems to know it, not even in UK. Do you have a brand or a link? Asked on the discords no one knew there either.

author: @peterfelecan3639
text:
FDM 3D printing works for prototyping; for production you need to use more resistant material,
BTW, printing at 100% infill will not solve the issue as the "grain" stays the same, at best it will shear a little bit later. Printing the pins separately, horizontally, at lower speed and probably higher temperature, ,using a better material such as PETG, can be a solution.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @onbedoeldekut1515
text:
(regarding 3D printing, Robert)
Is it inefficient, having to heat the entire bed when printing?
Might it be (even if only slightly) better to somehow heat the direct spot beneath the bed with something like a laser or even a wheel or heated current of air?
(I'm brand new to printing, and am waiting for my Neptune max).
You should definitely make some videos about customising printers, and what they could potentially do down the line.

Thanks again, Robert, I truly appreciate what you do.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it is mate - but the newer printers - including the Neptune 4s have zone heating now - however heating from the top will likely distort your print - i haven't done much on the printers themselves - i mostly just use them - but what you say makes sense - cheers mate

author: @LeighHowells
text: Very neat.  How well do 3d printed components last when used mechanically? Are you planning to use any of your turbines to generate at home, or is it all about the research / sharing?

replies.author: @LeighHowells
replies.text: Oh! I hadn't got to the sheared pin section when I wrote the above.  I was wondering how well cogs last turning against each other - or is this just for prototyping?

replies.author: @SinsBird
replies.text: @LeighHowells mostly depends on their size. You can make the cogs very big and do nothing else and they would last a long time. Or you can make them smaller but add water into the gearbox and this would prevent them softening from heat. Or you could add oil into the gearbox and it would cool them as well as improve lubrication. Or you could even electroplate them with metal.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i am a teacher at heart mate lol
